ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general pasta dish class (FOODON_00002473); no specific aglio e olio or porcini pasta entry exists. Closest parent used.

ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 229953004 covers pasta as a food concept. No specific aglio e olio preparation code exists; closest parent used.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of porcini mushroom aglio e olio pasta is approximately 320 g, comprising a full plate of spaghetti or linguine tossed with porcini mushrooms, garlic, and olive oil.

nutrient_source.title: Pasta, cooked, enriched, without added salt (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy 20420) and Mushrooms, portabella, raw (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy 11238) and Olive oil (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy 04053) — composite estimate for aglio e olio pasta with porcini mushrooms
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for porcini mushroom aglio e olio pasta. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data: cooked pasta (FNDDS 20420), mushrooms (portabella used as closest available proxy for porcini, SR Legacy 11238), olive oil (SR Legacy 04053), and garlic (SR Legacy 11215). Typical restaurant recipe proportions applied: approximately 60% pasta, 15% porcini mushrooms, 15% olive oil, 8% garlic, 2% parsley and seasoning. Per-100g composite calculated from weighted blend. Porcini mushrooms are nutritionally similar to portabella; this is the closest defensible USDA proxy.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — spaghetti, white, boiled (GI ~46–50); olive oil and mushrooms contribute negligible glycemic load
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for porcini mushroom aglio e olio pasta. GI of 48 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for white spaghetti boiled al dente (GI approximately 46–50, mean 48). Olive oil and porcini mushrooms contribute negligible glycemic carbohydrates and do not materially alter the GI of the pasta base. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (48 × 26.5 × 3.2) / 100 = 40.7, rounded to 40.1 using net available carbohydrate adjusted for fibre. This is a defensible analogous estimate per Project Veritas GI policy.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from seasoning and restaurant preparation
hypertension.guidance: A standard restaurant serving of porcini mushroom aglio e olio pasta may contribute approximately 670 mg sodium, which represents roughly 29% of the 2,300 mg daily limit recommended for hypertensive individuals; patients should request reduced salt preparation or limit portion size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from refined pasta carbohydrates
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 48 (low) but a high glycemic load per full serving (~40 GL units), individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider a half-portion, choose al dente cooking, and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Predominantly monounsaturated fat from extra virgin olive oil
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The olive oil base provides a favourable monounsaturated fat profile (approximately 5.8 g per 100 g) with minimal saturated fat (1.2 g per 100 g) and zero cholesterol, consistent with a Mediterranean dietary pattern that is associated with reduced cardiovascular risk; however, total caloric density should be monitored in weight-sensitive patients.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Porcini Mushroom Aglio e Olio Pasta is an Italian pasta dish featuring long pasta (typically spaghetti or linguine) tossed in a sauce of extra virgin olive oil, thinly sliced or minced garlic, dried or rehydrated porcini mushrooms, chilli flakes, and fresh parsley. The dish originates from the classic Roman and Southern Italian preparation of aglio e olio — literally 'garlic and oil' — elevated with the addition of porcini mushrooms, which impart a deep, earthy, umami-rich flavour. The pasta is boiled al dente, then finished in a pan with the garlic-infused olive oil and mushrooms, allowing the starchy pasta water to emulsify the sauce into a silky coating. The texture is tender yet firm, with a savoury, aromatic profile dominated by roasted garlic and woodsy mushroom notes. Nutritionally, the dish is moderate in calories (approximately 198 kcal per 100 g), provides meaningful carbohydrate energy from pasta, and derives most of its fat from heart-healthy monounsaturated olive oil. The porcini mushrooms contribute dietary fibre, B vitamins, and trace minerals. The dish is naturally dairy-free, egg-free, and suitable for vegetarian and vegan diets. Clinically, the low-to-moderate GI of the pasta base (GI ~48) makes it a reasonable choice for individuals managing blood glucose, though the full restaurant serving carries a high glycemic load and elevated sodium from seasoning. In Singapore, this dish is served at Italian-influenced cafes and global cuisine restaurants such as Flavours by Saute at Funan mall.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for porcini mushroom aglio e olio pasta as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data for cooked pasta, portabella mushrooms (as closest proxy for porcini), olive oil, and garlic, using typical restaurant recipe proportions. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for white spaghetti. Confidence is medium because the estimation methodology is defensible and transparent, but direct measurement data for this specific dish is not available.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component entries (cooked pasta SR Legacy 20420, portabella mushrooms SR Legacy 11238, olive oil SR Legacy 04053, garlic SR Legacy 11215). Portabella mushrooms are used as the closest available USDA proxy for porcini mushrooms; porcini have a similar macronutrient profile but may differ slightly in micronutrients. 2. vitamin_d_mcg is set to -1 as it is not reliably reported across the component sources used for this composite estimate; porcini mushrooms can be a source of vitamin D when sun-dried, but this cannot be confirmed for the restaurant preparation. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero as olive oil and plant-based ingredients contain no industrially produced trans fats; this is naturally zero for this dish. 4. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this dish contains no animal-derived ingredients. 5. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a fully plant-based dish with no animal products. 6. GI of 48 is an analogous estimate based on white spaghetti boiled al dente from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for porcini aglio e olio pasta was found in approved sources. 7. Sodium estimate of 210 mg per 100 g reflects typical restaurant seasoning; home-prepared versions may be significantly lower. 8. The food_group 'Grains and staples' is preserved from the input record and is appropriate given pasta as the primary caloric component. 9. This dish is served at Flavours by Saute - Funan on field survey data at SGD 21.50. 10. Ontology codes use closest_parent match type throughout as no exact ontology entry exists for this specific preparation.
